articles.title: Helen Webley-Brown
articles.contents:
Helen Webley-Brown
          
          
          
          
          
          About
          Research
          CV
          ☰
          
          
      
        
          
            
              
            
              Helen Webley-Brown
            
            
              
                
            
          
        
        
        
          
            I am a final-year S.M. student in Political Science at
            MIT with specializations in American Politics and
            Quantitative Methods. I am also an affiliate of the Center for American Political
            Studies at Harvard University. In 2022, I graduated
            from Washington University in St. Louis with a BA in
            Political Science.
            Although my research focuses on the US, I grew up in
            London, England. I moved across the pond for college via the
            US-UK Fulbright Commission’s wonderful Sutton Trust US
            Programme.
            My current research agenda fits into two broad
            buckets:
            Socio-political effects of interacting with the criminal legal system.
            How emerging
            technologies change the way people experience and
            perceive the state.
            At the intersection of this, I am interested in pre-trial
            electronic monitoring, and the role of tools like
            ShotSpotter and Flock in policing. I also have a nascent
            interest in how political violence targeted at elected
            officials impacts the representation of gender and racial
            minorities.
          
        
      
    

    
      
        
          
            
              
            
              Helen Webley-Brown
            
            
              
                
                                  
              
            
            
              I am a final-year S.M. student in Political Science at

              Quantitative Methods. I am also an affiliate of the Center for American Political
              Studies at Harvard University. In 2022, I
              graduated from Washington University in St. Louis with a
              BA in Political Science.
              Although my research focuses on the US, I grew up in
              London, England. I moved across the pond for college via
              the US-UK Fulbright Commission’s wonderful Sutton Trust US
              Programme.
              My current research agenda fits into two broad
              buckets:
              Socio-political effects of interacting with the criminal legal system.
              How emerging
              technologies change the way people experience and
              perceive the state.
              At the intersection of this, I am interested in
              pre-trial electronic monitoring, and the role of tools
              like ShotSpotter and Flock in policing. I also have a
              nascent interest in how political violence targeted at
              elected officials impacts the representation of gender and
              racial minorities.

            Helen Webley-Brown
          
          
            
              Hello! I am a final-year S.M. student in Political
              Science at MIT with specializations in American Politics
              and Quantitative Methods. My research focuses on carceral
              politics and the use of AI in state/local government
              services.
            
            
              Hello! I am a final-year S.M. student in Political
              Science at MIT with specializations in American Politics
              and Quantitative Methods. My research focuses on carceral
              politics and the use of AI in state/local government
              services.

articles.title: Research
articles.contents:
Conference Papers
Gupta, A., Wu, V., Webley-Brown, H., King, J., Ho, D. (2023). The Privacy-Bias Tradeoff: Data Minimization and Racial Disparity Assessments in U.S. Government. In Proceedings of the 2023 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT ’23).
 Best Paper Award – FAccT 2023
 Student Paper Award – Privacy Papers for Policymakers (PPPM) ; Future of Privacy Forum
 Policy Brief – Institute on Human-Centered
Artificial Intelligence (HAI)
Works in Progess
Democracy Under Surveillance: The Effect of Pretrial Electronic Monitoring on Voting Behavior
We’ve Got Democracy at Home: Resident Council Emergence in Public Housing Developments.
